title: Living in Boulder vlog 11-13-2018: History books

enrichment.summary: Kyle gives a Boulder vlog update explaining he's still working on his history books website (digitalizing/indexing audio books with mini-chapters), covering a 3,200-page Months book, the Old and New Testament, and a Book of Job. He discusses his next big project—building Rome onto Google Earth mapped to Boulder County—and shares enthusiastic tangents about Aristotle's Politics and Plato's Republic as research for a requested monarchy video.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: One of the best books ever written; ancient Greeks understood political systems better than we do today
Too many are unrealistic and grandiose; needs more intelligent, realistic dialogue
Most people waste their lives in polluting industries; better to live simply, study history, and build meaningful work
Once he fixes all broken links, the site will go viral organically without marketing
Not afraid to start new ones because he knows he can finish each one given enough time
Breaking books into mini-chapters helps even uninterested or distracted people engage with difficult material

transcript.text: Hello, Living in Boulder V-Log for 1 p.m. on November 13th, 2018.
YouTube says I haven't done a video for three weeks.
I wanted to do a video after two weeks, but I remember the last video that I did on the 22nd of October.
I predicted that I would be done tying up the loose ends on my history books page, which is on my education page.
But three weeks comes by, and I'm still doing work.
I didn't realize how much work there was, and so that's why I was like, oh, let's do a video when I'm done, and then I realize I'm not going to be done.
So right now I'm working on Momsen.
I looked at 3R Momsen, and I noticed that it wasn't at the same level as the other books.
I built it a bad way.
I guess I got lazy.
Maybe it was one of the first books I did, or maybe I did it with the other computer.
It was too slow because it was too big.
It says it's 3,200 pages.
But, yeah, I'm breaking up all the different chapters in it, and so I rebuilt it, the body of it, and so now I'm building the menu part.
And that's super interesting for me because that's how I learn a book.
I have to have that menu.
I have to be looking at that menu of all the different little mini chapters to know because then you can zero in, you know.
Even with the cell phone, you look it through it, and you go, ooh, that's a good one, and then you click it, and it's only like a minute.
They're broken up into little sections of a page.
But that means if it's 3,200 pages on there, a page each, I have to, you know, do through all the steps, find out how many pages there are in time for 3,200 chapters.
And so then I'm going to do the Old Testament, and that has, like, 972 chapters because I don't have that done on it.
And then I'm going to do the New Testament because that doesn't have it.
And then I have to do another sweep through to make sure all the books on my page are up to snuff because there could be more books.
I think I clicked on Seneca, and that one looked messed up.
So, you know, just make sure they're all up to the same level.
And then I could get going back on building the law code, which is one of those huge projects.
I think it's 26,000 pages is what they say.
So, ooh.
But, yeah, that'd be cool to find out exactly.
And then because then you could have the opportunity to have the computer to read it to you.
I'm doing my video right now with my new computer.
I used to do it with my old computer, but my old computer won't turn on.
That happened since the last time I did a video three weeks ago because I – but, yeah, so I got a meeting with – because it just wouldn't get any juice.
It woke up dead, and it wouldn't get juice.
I have three chargers.
And so I tried all three of my chargers in all the different outlets in the house and would not get a charge.
So I took it to the store, and the guy plugged it in, and it got a charge.
And he's like, yeah, that's because your cable's bad.
So I guess I'm going to go into the store with all three of my cables and plug it in, and then if none of them work in the store, I'll just be like, buy me another cable.
So – but I never did anything to these cables, like, ever.
I just bought them and plugged them in.
And I didn't – but, anyway, so somebody wanted me to do a video about monarchy, and so I got super excited.
I love it when people ask me to do videos, intelligent videos.
A couple years ago, the other kinds of videos people were asking me were like, what do you think about pizza?
What do you think about ice cream?
And I was doing them.
Because I had this rule, I'll do anything you request for to.
And – but, yeah, that video.
But I want to take that video seriously, so I started listening to Aristotle Politics, Plato, Republic.
But then, you know, all of Aristotle and Plato, or Plato – then you have Plato Laws and all – yeah, all the dialogue.
So it's a lot of reading, and so I was – I got those books up to snuff.
If those weren't – and so, yeah, I can go to – Aristotle Politics is probably the best book you could ever read.
You should check it out on my website.
You – because you can look at the menu, and then you just go, ooh, that looks interesting, and you click on it, and it reads to you.
I don't even want to get into it, because that's the video about monarchy.
But I should probably not be too high when I do that video.
I'm super high now.
So I got up – I was working until, like, 5.
I usually work until, like, 8, but I worked until, like, 5, and then I just got – I wasn't really sleepy, but I just got worn out.
And – but then I woke up at, like, 11.
I started working.
And – and then – and then I decided, yeah, yeah, let's do a video, because I'm kind of nowhere near being done.

I might do a couple more books, the Anti-Federal Papers, and – but, yeah, no, but I should probably – I think I want to move on and finish Google Earth.

I want to finish Google Earthing Rome, the city of Rome, so I can do YouTube videos of it.
Yeah, you know what? Talking about it is actually making me decide.
That's definitely what I want to do.
And the same thing with Boulder, and, you know, you can do it history, too.
I want to do the entire history of both of those.
And then what I'm going to do is I'm going to build Rome on Boulder.

So – and all the links worked, and it was fine.

But – so, okay, so let's do predictions.

This time, I'm going to wait two weeks to do a video.

I think in the very beginning I'm doing – the last thing that I was doing on Google Earth was –

Anybody who travels around Boulder County all through the trails and the mountains,
Mountains, they're traveling – they can travel around the kingdom of Rome as they're doing that.

And I even have a – you know, there's also my idea of this show, Boulder Rome,
Where you retell the story and you increase speed 50 times.

So if you're at the day where there's a battle somewhere else, then you ride your bike out there

And then that gives us all kinds of creative opportunities.
Like you can have conversations, you know, historical conversations.
One thing that really bothers me about – and it's getting a lot better.
Like I saw the – what's it called? The Rebel King, Outlaw King on Netflix,
And that was super realistic.
They had everything, you know.
They tried to make it appeased, make the historians happy because too much of the time,
The historical movies, they just – there's too many fantasy history movies.

And it's about time we have conversations between people in historical movies
Where they're as real life as possible and the people are serious
And they're intelligent people.
And so that's what I want to do with my history.
You know, to even do the little things like the border disputes
Between the peasants on the edges of the different kingdoms,
Which got Albalong and Rome and their battle.
So anyways, that's going to be for a while.
I think I'm realizing – I'm starting to kind of realize that I'm in deep water.
And then just here – I think I told my fiancée three years,
And she's like, cool, 30 more years.
It kind of doesn't matter, you know,
Because the fact of the matter is that there's 7 billion people in the world

They just – they just – to change the course of our trajectory towards death.
They just work in an industry that pollutes, you know,
A planet or something that we don't need to do.
Like, we don't need any more of.
What we need more of is to slim down and to try to live like they did in the past
Without technology or we're just happy enough eating kale
And riding our bike throughout the day and listening to it in P3.
And that's it.
You do it your whole life, and then you come home
And you make everybody else smarter.

And I just turn into Jabba the Hutt,
Then that's actually a success, you know.

Especially with the computers they are these days,
They're fast enough these days,

Because, you know, one thing you need is time,

Walking around and alive and happy and da-da-da-da-da-da.

Before my attitude changes, you know,

I probably thought to myself,

Like memorize it.

As high as I can get, and then working on my website,
Which further reduces my brain cells.

Because then you would, like, not absorb all of it and stuff.

Because it's broken up in all the different little mini chapters.

And then you just listen to them one at a time.

Because that kind of – it would bring my intelligence level down to the level of the average human
Who doesn't even want to learn at all.

I think because they're overwhelmed by all the pages and they just –
So you have to draw them in.
So you do that by making them feel like they can – there's an in.
There's a way for them to start learning.

And then – or listening to, like, certain chapters of different various sizes

The ones that are interesting.

You would then see – you would Google, you know, whoever they mention.
You would – Wikipedia it.
A lot of – most of the guys that Aristotle and Plato were mentioning

But it's amazing.
Politics is amazing because he talks – he has all these stories of – he has all the different – okay, I'll say it really fast.
This is for another video.
But Aristotle politics is really interesting because he has all the different political structures.
But then he has all the causes for how they change and how each of them disintegrates and why there's revolutions.
And he has examples, all these different examples.
For example, they were talking about how there was this one town that – and they – and the city took in a bunch of refugees from another town who weren't like them,
Didn't speak their language, totally different race or whatever.
I don't know, but different – different city-state.
Yeah, but – and then the refugees came in and took over the whole place and then kicked the original guys out.
And he mentioned, like, a half dozen of those.
And so I want to find that one again.

It's all kinds of stuff like that.
But – but, yeah, and then mentioning all the different political systems of all the different towns, city-states.
You know, each city-state had its own political system.
So they knew, you know, like they knew way more than we did.
You know, people might be like, oh, there's still politics so long ago.
No, no, no.
That guy knew more than we do.
They were like a living petri dish of political structures back in those days.
The ones that everybody took – respected the most and took their – took their cues from were the Spartans and the Cretans.

So that's maybe why Romans were Spartan, too.

It's amazing.
That book is amazing.

The Republic.
